sentences.text: Benjamin Kawer.
Last name is spelled K-A-W-E-R.
My first name has never been changed, and it's B-E-N-J-A-M-I-N.
In any language it was the same spelling.
That was my name at birth.
No, with the exception that during the war I was called-- when I arrived in Belgium I was called Beno-- B-E-N-O, strictly an abbreviation for Ben in Belgium and--
I was born in Ostroleka, spelled O-S-T-R-O-L-E-N-K-A.
That was Poland.
Well, our family consisted of-- I had two sisters and one brother, father and mother.
I was the youngest one of four children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I left-- my parents left the city where I was born when I was two years old, which makes it approximately 1927.
They moved to another city called Hajnowka, which was in Poland.
It was about 180 kilometers away from the first city where I was born.
And this is where my parents made their home.
They have moved there simply because my grandfather, my father's father, moved there about a couple of years before that.
And I guess the opportunities were there.
My grandfather moved there also simply because his father, who was a very wealthy man before the Russian Revolution-- of course, he has lost everything.

And he told his son, which is my grandfather, Moshe, we are moving to the new frontiers-- this is after Poland has gained their independence in about 1918-- because there are better opportunities there.
And this is where my-- this is when my grandfather left in about 19-- probably-- 26 or so or '25, and my father followed-- my father and his brother, both of them.
And there were two unmarried daughters, the girls-- I mean, my-- my grandfather's children, who also moved to the same city.
And my father worked in-- in the forest, since this city was right on the outskirts of one of the largest forests in Poland called Puszcza Bialowieza.
And new industries started there.
They had the largest lumber mill in the country, which was happen to have been in that particular city.
So there were a lot of new people.
It was a small city, but it's like the pioneer towns where industry came in.
Other people moved in in there, and my parents were one of them.

So after working for a while in the forest as a lumber hauler-- he was taking out the woods out of the forest onto the railroad tracks and from there it went to the mills-- he started thinking about going in business for himself.
And he slowly build [BACKGROUND NOISE] a brick factory.
So since about 19-- I would say-- 29 or so, we were-- my father had a brick factory manufacturing bricks, not like they do it in here.

As a matter of fact, after watching the-- the movie and how the Jews worked in Egypt, this is exactly the picture which I remember how-- how this particular factory started, where the people-- people were actually stumping their feet on the clay and just mixing it to make it softer so they can-- so they can produce bricks.
And slowly, of course, progress even reached our little town, and they had a machine to do all the mixing of the clay and-- and instead of people doing it.
And-- and no electricity, but it was done with-- with horses.
They were-- they were going around in a circle, and-- and-- and-- and this is how he made his living in that town in there.
The town was approximately-- before the war, before 1939, we had approximately 25,000 population spread out, quite a bit spread out.
It was small, but-- but the Jewish population at that time, we had about 120 families.
We had one synagogue.
And of course I never knew that there was any other synagogue besides an Orthodox synagogue, so of course I was brought up in an Orthodox way.
I probably attended Hebrew school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the time I probably could start walking, maybe at three years of age.
And my secular education stopped when I was 11.
I never attended a strictly Jewish school.
I went to public school.
But after school, of course, I used to go to Hebrew school.
So all of my religious education stopped at 11 years of age.

So whatever I have acquired was until 11 years of age, because at that time I stopped simply because the public school was more demanding, and I was very active in extracurricular activity, in school, too, singing in a choir, sports activities.
So of course, I-- I-- I didn't have the time or-- or really the desire to go and continue with the Jewish education.
Somehow, though, I have retained must-- most of my secular education.
And although I've never had another Hebrew lesson since I was 11 years of age, I-- I know my Bible quite well.
I know my Hebrew reading and my prayers.
I retained it all with me.
I'm even surprised myself.
But I guess when you learn when you're young, your mind absorbs anything and everything.
It's like a sponge.
And I probably was a good student, so.
Excuse me for one second.
[DRINKS] My father's name was [HEBREW].
He was called Hersh Leib.
Most of the Jewish names are actually Hebrew names.
So his Hebrew name was [HEBREW], but he was called Hersh.
My mother's name was Dyna.
I had-- my oldest sister's name was Rochell, which is Rachel.
My brother, which was next-- excuse me, but I'm closing up.
My throat is closing up.
This is what happens when I talk about it.
[DRINKS] My brother's name was-- since he passed away about five years ago, [?
Lazer, ?] or Eliezer in Hebrew.
I was the youngest, as I said, of four children.
And my-- and I had a sister, which was just one year older than I was.
Her name was Ester.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We lived, of course, in that town until the war from 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] My grandfather's name [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was Moses.
His name was actually Moshe [HEBREW].
My grandmother's name was Faige.
My grandfather died, I'd say, approximately in 1929.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember it because he called me-- and I was his grandson-- and he said, I want-- I want you to go and call Bubbe and tell her that I'm going to die.
And this-- I didn't even understand what dying meant, I guess, at that time.
But I retained this.
I mean, I-- I have a picture of this, remembering it.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And he died that particular day also.
My grandma, Faige, or Faige as they called her, she died about, I'd say, two years after my grandfather died.
I never knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the grandparents on my mother's side.
They all undoubtedly passed away before I was born.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the war started in September of 1939.
Unfortunately, it didn't last long, but we suffered a casualty the first few days of the war.
My relatives, my cousins, under the name of Gertzek, lived near railroads.
And since the Germans were bombing the railroad tracks-- they lived near the railroad tracks-- they came over to our house, because we lived a little bit away from everything.
And the Polish artillery decided to make a stop about a kilometer away from our-- from our place in a small little mill.
And the German tanks came in, and our home was exactly in their fire lines.
We were all in the house with the shutters closed.
We're laying on the floor.
And one of the shells, tank shells, went through our home, and it started smoking.
So my Uncle Abraham, he ran out of the house, and he was immediately machine gunned by the Germans and he died.
The Germans were in town for about maybe five, six days.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I guess Hitler and Stalin signed the pact where they would divide Poland.
So the Germans moved out after a few days.
And this was around [BACKGROUND NOISE] just before our holiday, which is right now approaching Yom Kippur.
As they moved out, I remember we gathered in one of the homes, not in the synagogue, and to recite the prayers.
And then somebody came into the house and indicated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Germans are returning.
Didn't know why.
So my parents said to me, run to the factory.
The factory was about three miles out of town.
You go there.
They remained in the city.
And I remember that night.
We did not have telephone, call each other.
And I remember that night.
Even though I ran to the factory, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had my prayer book.
And I remember this very vividly.
I recited [HEBREW] [SIGHS] in the moonlight.
The following day, my father came to the factory.
He said, they did not return.
It was just a rumor that they came back, that they're coming back.
They did not.
And then, of course, a few days later, the Russians came in and occupied our town.
And we were under the-- on the Russian side of Poland, which was divided into two sections.
One the Germans kept, and we were on the other section.
And I was there attending Russian school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They called it there the gymnasium.
This is, I presume, on the level of high school, but was probably a little bit more strict or more-- the curriculum was a little bit higher than-- than the high school in there.
And I attended the Russian school for approximately 23 or 21 months in Russian school.
It wasn't too bad, with the exception that they have-- for me, as a youngster, it was great.
I was only 14 years old at the time in 1939.
And suddenly, I was on an equal basis with everyone-- with everyone else.
The Polish kids, of course, they always called names-- dirty Jew and everything else.
Suddenly the Russians came in, we were supposed to be equal.
So as far as I was concerned, as a young boy, I wasn't afraid anymore of the-- of the Polish students, and we were on an equal basis.
However, I learned very, very fast, of course, since we were-- we owned a factory, we were industrialists supposedly.
So we were outcasts again, because the Russians didn't like anyone who supposedly was a capitalist.
We were far from being wealthy or capitalist, but they didn't like it.
And then I realized that my father was really suffering.
They closed down the factory.
As a matter of fact, it wasn't functioning.
And then one day, I think it was in 1940 or so, towards the middle of 1940, they just had no one to run the factory, or they didn't know how to run the factory.
So they came and they asked my father to start up as a worker, just working there.
And I remember my father took me on that little tripped horse and-- and wagon just to the factory.
And-- and I remember him crying in a corner.
He said, I built this.
And he did, with his own-- with his own hands, he build that factory.
And now they're asking me just to work for them, to be a salaried man.
But nevertheless, there's not much you could do anyway.
So he-- we worked in there.
He worked in there.
And of course, I remember as-- as a boy, eight, nine years old, I was working in the factory there all the time helping out, too.
My job was to load the-- the-- the bricks onto a wagon, and-- and then the wagons would go wherever the brick had to be delivered.
And we-- since we had our own horses also, so many times that I was doing this.
And I was with the horses and loading and then unloading the-- the bricks.
And this continued, of course, until 1941, when the war started with Germany.
And it didn't take long for the Germans to come into our town and occupy it.
And of course, they immediately picked on the Jews.
They immediately gathered us to a place and demanding things which they knew were impossible, like they need 100 pounds of pepper, 100 pounds of cocoa.
Those are spices, I mean, which didn't even grow in-- in Poland, and they had to deliver it.
I mean, everybody emptied out whatever they had and, of course, to bring all the gold and silver and everything else.

And it didn't take long, and [BACKGROUND NOISE] we were evacuated from our town, Hajnowka, to a ghetto, Pruzana, which was closer to the Russian border, the part of Poland that we lived during the Russian occupation, which was the same town, but it was called White Russia, which is today, I guess, White Russia, or Belarus they call it.
Now, the evacuation-- the evacuation of-- of the city, just take whatever you can, so how much can you carry on you.
And we-- we were marched for about five kilometers into the highway, which was in the forest on both sides.
During that little march, they managed to kill our, well, our rabbi, whose name-- I do not remember the first name.
I have a document somewheres, which I couldn't put my hands on now.
His name was Kopf, K-O-P-F, Kopf.
He was a very strong man.
And during that march, they-- the rabbi had to carry the physician on his back, was a Jewish physician.
And they had the-- the rabbi dig a grave.
And what I witnessed is, they took a shovel, the shovel which the rabbi was digging the grave, and they just chopped off the skull of the doctor and killed him right there.
And then they shot the rabbi, and they-- and of course, we burn him.
And then they put us on trucks, and they took us to that ghetto Pruzana, P-I-U-Z-A-N-Y.
And of course, the Jewish community in that ghetto, they just put us in a home, and every room contained a family.

So we were-- we were five of us-- excuse me, it was four of us-- my mother and father, myself, and my sister-- four of us, we were in that one room, because there were probably five, six other families in the same house.
They all had a room.
So there wasn't much space in there.
In that camp, of course, we were getting certain rations, not enough to die, but not enough to live either.
And then I was taken to a camp to build a highway in the forest.
So I was taken away from the family, even though we were in that camp, in the ghetto.
I was-- I was taken away, and I worked in there-- it was most of the summer that I worked in that camp.
And-- and it could have been 1942.
I really do not recall [BACKGROUND NOISE] the exact dates.
I remember my father visited me once, because they have-- the Germans have killed one guy.
They have actually taken three guys out just to execute them.
There was no reason for it.
But they-- two of them escaped.
One was killed.
And this two made their way back to the ghetto.
And of course, when they came, they said, probably by now everybody is shot.
So when my father came-- he was delivering food to our camp.
So when he came there, I had a lot of clothes, dirty clothes.
I send it back home.
And of course, when he brought the clothes, my whole family, he's dead, he's dead.
Just like-- this is his clothes was returned.
And anyway, my father finally convinced them I'm alive.
And when the job was finished, they took us all back to the ghetto.
And of course, this was-- I would assume it was late in 1940-- in 19-- had to be 1942.
In that ghetto, my sister was still there.
My married sister with two children was there.
My brother, who's a baby boy, was born in the ghetto.
He had two-- my brother had two children.
My-- my brother had three children.
He had two girls and a boy.
My older sister had a boy and a girl.
Of course, in about the end of January we were taken to the train station in-- from that city.
It was quite a way.
And we were loaded into the boxcars [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the trip, which we didn't know, of course, then to Auschwitz.
And I remember that last night.
We knew we were being evacuated.
Really, we did not know where exactly.
And I remember the day.
I went with the third transport.
There were four transports going-- leaving the city.
I went with the third transport.
So it would have to be probably about January 30, January 31, or January 29 of 1943.
But I remember as we were leaving the ghetto, I saw a young man jumping from roof to roof with a gun in his hands, shooting.
Of course, he never had a chance.
He was only one.
Maybe there were two more, I don't know.
But I only saw one.
And of course, they gunned him down right there, fell off the roof.
And this was the first-- I mean, I've seen many casualties.
This was the first one that I witnessed, really, besides the one on the march from our city where they killed the rabbi and-- and the doctor-- and the physician.
And of course, we-- they loaded us in boxcars-- boxcars.
And-- and-- and my father, mother, and we all happened to have been-- we all went with the same transport.
So we were all in the same car, boxcar.
I remember-- I do not recall the days or nights that it took to get to Auschwitz-- but I remember that my father turned completely white in the boxcar.
I mean, he was a young man.
My father, at the time, was 55 years old, and so was my mother.
But they both turned white.
And of course, when we arrived in Auschwitz, the selection started, because the-- the-- the-- the-- the noises, the voices, the yelling-- aus, raus, raus, raus.
And immediately they started beating us, immediately.
They had canes in their-- in their hands, and they started beating us already.
The one [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] story, the one-- the one action which I have witnessed in there-- there was a very, very old couple.
Of course-- of course, when I arrived in-- in-- in Auschwitz in 1943, I was 17 years old, January of 1943.
So I really-- almost anyone older than I was old.
And they just wouldn't part.
They were just holding down to each other, holding onto each other.
And they were beating them with the canes over their heads.
You could see the blood on the white hair.
They just wouldn't-- and finally they took canes with a-- with a hook at the end, and they just-- around their necks, and they just pulled them apart.
This is the arrival.
This is what I have seen the first minute after-- after jumping out of the-- of the boxcars.
Now, of course, they separated immediately the men, women, and children.
My sister, of course, wouldn't part with her two kids.
One was still a baby, an infant.
Of course, she went on a truck with the women.
My sister-in-law, my brother's wife, was the same thing.
And of course, my mother, I guess, took one of the children, helping out daughter.
And-- and-- and they were loaded onto the trucks.
Now, my father and my brother and I were three.
We were set up to be three in a row.
I could assess the situation immediately what was going on in there.
I mean, they were taking away the kids.
They were taking away the women.
They were taking away the-- all the people on one side.
And at that time, my father said to my brother, he said, remember, keep him always with you.
He is the youngest.
And I-- I probably couldn't imagine or dreaming that they would just take the people and-- and put them in gas chambers right away.
But I realized the situation when it came to the-- they call it selections-- right, left, right, left.
They have sent me with-- with my father.
And my father told me-- and I realized the situation too-- get over to the other side.
Go where your brother is.
And I have jumped over.
At 17, I was as tall, and-- and-- and I had the build, the same build as I am now.
I mean, I was 5' 11' at the time.
And-- and so I could pass as a worker.
I guess the face was still young.
And somehow I was accepted.
Nobody came back running after me.
And I was in-- in-- taken in to the labor camp, of course, while my father went the other way.
And this was, of course, the last time I have seen my father or mother.
I seen him go away in the trucks.
And this was the-- the beginning of concentration camp.
This was Birkenau.
We were taken from there to Birkenau, because we went through the-- the undressing and-- and-- and everything else and the disinfection, what they call, and disinfected us all.
It could have been a gas chamber.
What did we know where they were taking us in there?
And this is when-- Birkenau.
They took us all to Birkenau from there into barracks, and they gave us the striped clothes, wooden shoes.
We were stripped of everything.
I remember I did hide some pictures.
It's the only thing which I was able to hide-- some pictures inside my body, inside my buttock, as a matter of fact, and I brought them in to camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just to say briefly what happened to the pictures, of course, one of--
